expectations.text: The review identifies at least one concrete issue with file and line reference
expectations.evidence: The review contains 21 issues each with a 'file' and 'line' field. Examples: colorizer.py:977, colorbar.py:437, figure.py:1275, etc.

expectations.text: Every critical or warning issue includes a specific suggested fix, not just a description of the problem
expectations.evidence: All issues include actionable suggestions: e.g., colorizer.py:977 says 'add a FutureWarning in this release, change behavior in 3.N+2'; colorbar.py:437 says 'replace self.ax with appropriate logic using self.axes'; colorbar.py:99 says 'Use an immutable default instead: ticklocations=(auto, auto) (tuple) or ticklocations=None with an internal default'. One issue (axes/_axes.py:6660) is borderline — it says 'Verify this does not produce incorrect vmin/vmax' — more of a flag than a fix, but provides enough guidance.

expectations.text: The review does not contain false positives (flagged issues that are actually correct code)
expectations.evidence: Critical issue #4 (colorbar.py:304) claims 'the bivariate colorbar will NOT redraw' because `#self._draw_all()` is commented out in `update_normals`. However, `update_normals` calls `self.set_alpha(self.mappable.get_alpha())`, and `set_alpha` unconditionally calls `self._draw_all()` (confirmed in diff). The colorbar DOES redraw on every `update_normals` call. The review's claim that 'the colorbar will show stale colors' is factually incorrect — this is a false positive. The `self.ax` AttributeError claim in colorbar.py:437 is also partially misstated: the AttributeError IS caught by a try/except block, so it won't cause an unhandled exception; but the caught exception causes incorrect behavior (treats gridspec=True as gridspec=False). The bug is real but the failure mode description is wrong.

expectations.text: The review checks code changes against repo-specific conventions from the skill
expectations.evidence: The review explicitly checks: (1) deprecation protocol with _api.warn_deprecated and FutureWarning; (2) NumPy-style docstrings enforced by ruff D rules; (3) .pyi stub files for colorbar.pyi and figure.pyi; (4) doc/api/next_api_changes/behavior_changes/ RST entries; (5) doc/release/next_whats_new/ RST files; (6) mutable default arguments; (7) error type conventions using _api helpers.

expectations.text: The review verdict (APPROVE/REQUEST_CHANGES/COMMENT) is appropriate to the severity of issues found
expectations.evidence: Verdict is REQUEST_CHANGES. The review identifies 4 critical bugs including runtime AttributeError in MultivarColorbar.remove(), undeprecated API return-type breaks in Colorizer.get_clim() and clip property (confirmed in diff: scalar norm now returns 1-tuples instead of plain floats/bools), and multiple missing stubs/docstrings. REQUEST_CHANGES is the appropriate verdict.

expectations.text: The review was posted to GitHub as a formal review with inline comments
expectations.evidence: mcp__github__get_pull_request_reviews returned [] and mcp__github__get_pull_request_comments also returned []. No review or inline comments were posted to GitHub. The review exists only as a local review.json file.

expectations.text: All files changed in PR #31214 were examined before forming conclusions
expectations.evidence: The PR has 30 changed files. The review only references issues in ~9 files (colorizer.py, colorbar.py, figure.py, colorbar.pyi, figure.pyi, axes/_axes.py, image.py, tests/test_colorbar.py, doc/release/next_whats_new/). Files like colors.py, pyplot.py, colorizer.pyi, collections.py, backend_bases.py, axes/_axes.pyi, colors.pyi, and multiple baseline image files are not mentioned. No transcript exists to verify whether those files were examined and found issue-free. The colorizer.pyi diff shows substantial API changes (Colorizer.get_clim return type stub updated to tuple[tuple[float|None,...], ...]) that could warrant a comment but none was made.

claims.claim: MultivarColorbar.remove() references self.ax which doesn't exist, causing AttributeError at runtime
claims.evidence: Confirmed in diff: MultivarColorbar.remove() contains `self.ax.get_subplotspec()` but the class only sets `self.axes` (a list). The AttributeError IS caught by the surrounding try/except block, so it doesn't crash; instead it silently treats gridspec=True as gridspec=False — still a real bug, though less severe than claimed.

claims.claim: Colorizer.get_clim() return type is silently changed for scalar norms to return 1-tuples
claims.evidence: Confirmed in colorizer.py diff: old code was `return self.norm.vmin, self.norm.vmax`; new code for n_components==1 is `return (self.norm.vmin, ), (self.norm.vmax, )`.

claims.claim: Colorizer.clip property return type changed to a 1-tuple for scalar norms
claims.evidence: Confirmed in colorizer.py diff: `return (self.norm.clip, )` for n_components==1. This is a behavior change: `(False,)` is truthy while `False` is falsy, so user code doing `if not colorizer.clip:` would break.
Confirmed in colorbar.py diff: `class MultivarColorbar(Sequence):` is followed immediately by `def __init__(self, axes, mappable=None, **kwargs):` with no class-level docstring.
Confirmed in colorbar.pyi diff: BivarColorbar stub was added but no MultivarColorbar stub appears anywhere in the patch.
Confirmed in figure.pyi diff: only `bivar_colorbar` was added to FigureBase; no `multivar_colorbar` stub.

claims.claim: No what's-new RST file was added
claims.evidence: PR changes 30 files; none are in doc/release/next_whats_new/.

claims.claim: test_bivar_colorbar_location is parametrized only with use_gridspec=True

claims.evidence: Confirmed in colorbar.py diff: `The second element must be {'auto', 'top', 'bottom''}` has double single-quote.

claims.claim: String concatenation in error message produces 'ascalar colormap'
claims.evidence: Confirmed in figure.py diff: `raise ValueError('colorbar can only be together with a' + 'scalar colormap')` — missing space between 'a' and 'scalar'.

claims.claim: BivarColorbar.update_normals() will NOT redraw the colorbar because _draw_all() is commented out
claims.evidence: This claim is INCORRECT. `update_normals` calls `self.set_alpha(self.mappable.get_alpha())` and `set_alpha` unconditionally calls `self._draw_all()`. The commented-out `#self._draw_all()` in update_normals is simply redundant, not a missing call.

claims.claim: Mutable default argument ticklocations=['auto', 'auto'] in BivarColorbar.__init__
claims.evidence: Confirmed in colorbar.py diff: `ticklocations=['auto', 'auto']` is a mutable list default.

suggestions.assertion: The review does not contain false positives (flagged issues that are actually correct code)
suggestions.reason: This expectation is hard to grade without reading the full diff — a grader relying solely on the review text would miss that set_alpha always calls _draw_all(). The expectation is appropriate but requires deep code analysis. Consider also checking whether the self.ax AttributeError claim is accurate about the failure mode (it's caught, causing wrong behavior, not an unhandled crash).

No assertion checks whether the review missed real bugs in files that were changed but not mentioned. The colorizer.pyi shows that Colorizer.vmin/vmax stubs were changed to return tuples — if this is wrong it would be a type-level bug. pyplot.py adds bivar_colorbar but multivar_colorbar is absent — consistent with the text but not independently verified from files.

suggestions.assertion: The review was posted to GitHub as a formal review with inline comments
suggestions.reason: This is a critical expectation for a 'PR review' task and is easily verified via the GitHub API. FAIL here is unambiguous and important — it means the review had no real-world impact on the PR.

eval_feedback.overall: Core correctness assertions (file/line references, suggested fixes, verdict) are well-designed. The false-positive check is the hardest to grade but most discriminating. The GitHub posting check is crucial and easily verified. Consider adding an assertion that checks whether the review correctly identifies at least N files from the diff (to catch shallow reviews that only look at a few files).
